---
title: ""
output: 
  html_document: 
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

#

<center> ![A picture of me at work](me25.jpg) </center>

$$\Huge\text{Bob Week}$$

$$\small{\sim\text{Mathematical Evolutionary Ecology}\sim}$$

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am interested in understanding the relationship between pattern and process in ecological communities. This inquiry leads to a broad range of questions involving mechanisms for species coexistence, the role of contemporary coevolution, the structure of interaction networks, the effects of genetic constraints on community level patterns, feedbacks with ecosystem processes and the macroevolutionary history of interacting taxa. However, developing models and statistical methods to explore these areas leads to challenging technical problems. I use my background in mathematics and intuition gained from studying plant-pollinator interactions to confront these challenges and contribute to collaborations with evolutionary biologists, ecologists and mathematicians.
</b>
</p>

### Current position

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
I am a PhD candidate at the University of Idaho advised by Professor [Scott Nuismer](https://www.leeef.org) with plans to defend in June 2020. My degree is in Bioinformatics and Computational Biology ([BCB](https://www.uidaho.edu/sci/bcb)), an interdisciplinary PhD program closely affiliated with The Institute for Bioinformatics and Evolutionary Studies ([IBEST](http://www.ibest.uidaho.edu/)). My CV is available [here](bob_week.pdf).
</b>
</p>

### PhD work

<p align=justify style="background-color:#2E2E2E;
border-radius:4px;
font-size:16px;
padding:15px;
margin:5px;"><b>
My dissertation contributes to a coevolutionary theory of community ecology with a focus on plant-pollinator networks. In particular, I have recently developed a Maximum Likelihood method to infer the strength of coevolution between pairs of species using spatially structured phenotypic data ([Week & Nuismer 2019](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/ele.13231)). I am currently finishing a project applying Diffusion Limits of Measure-Valued Branching Processes (e.g., [super-Brownian motion](http://www.math.ubc.ca/~perkins/superbrownianmotionandcriticalspatialsystems.pdf)) and their associated Stochastic Partial Differential Equations ([SPDE](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stochastic_partial_differential_equation)) to formally derive stochastic models of coevolving communities.
</b>
</p>
